factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Bright yellow daisy-like heads with a dark raised center make this one of the easiest summer prairie flowers to recognize.
leafDescription: Leaves are rough, hairy, and mostly lance-shaped, with broader lower leaves and narrower upper leaves.
flowerDescription: Each stem usually carries a single flower head with golden rays around a dark brown disk.
habitatDescription: Found in prairies, openings, savannas, and sunny disturbed ground across much of Illinois.
notes: Short-lived but quick to establish, especially in restored prairies and roadside plantings.

images.photographer: Dr. Thomas G. Barnes, U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service
images.caption: Close-up flower and bud view used as the primary offline field image.
Seed head / fruit of black eyed susan (formerly labeled detail).
Flower detail of black eyed susan.
Leaf detail of black eyed susan.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: A wetland iris with fans of sword-like leaves and blue-violet blossoms held just above the foliage.
leafDescription: Leaves are long, flat, and strongly sword-shaped, rising from the base in dense fans.
flowerDescription: Flowers are blue to violet with broad drooping sepals and lighter signal markings near the throat.
habitatDescription: Most often found in wet meadows, marsh edges, sedge meadows, and along shallow water margins.
notes: A reliable indicator of persistent moisture and one of the most recognizable native wetland flowers.

images.caption: Blue flag iris (Iris virginica var. shrevei) in bloom.
Close-up flower detail of blue flag iris.
Seed head / fruit of blue flag iris.
Leaf detail of blue flag iris.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Clusters of brilliant orange flowers and narrow alternate leaves make this milkweed stand out immediately.
leafDescription: Leaves are narrow to lance-shaped, usually alternate, and lack the broad opposite pattern common in other milkweeds.
flowerDescription: Flower clusters are dense and upright, typically vivid orange with occasional yellow tones.
habitatDescription: Thrives in dry prairies, rocky openings, and sunny roadside remnants with well-drained soil.
notes: A favorite for monarch habitat plantings, though it usually appears shorter and tidier than common milkweed.

images.caption: Butterfly milkweed with bright orange flower clusters.
Close-up flower detail of butterfly milkweed.
Seed head / fruit of butterfly milkweed.
Leaf detail of butterfly milkweed.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Tall spikes of intense scarlet flowers rise above wet ground in late summer and are hard to mistake.
leafDescription: Leaves are lance-shaped, toothed, and arranged alternately along an upright stem.
flowerDescription: The flowers are tubular and bright red, arranged along one side of the spike with protruding stamens.
habitatDescription: Common in wet ditches, streambanks, prairie swales, and other sunny to lightly shaded wet places.
notes: A standout late-season species that draws hummingbirds and anchors wetland pollinator gardens.

images.caption: Cardinal flower in bloom with a close view of the scarlet spike.
Close-up flower detail of cardinal flower.
Seed head / fruit of cardinal flower.
Leaf detail of cardinal flower.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Large purple-pink flower heads with drooping rays and a spiny orange-brown cone make this species easy to spot.
leafDescription: Basal leaves are broad and rough while stem leaves become narrower upward; all feel coarse to the touch.
flowerDescription: Each flower head shows long drooping ray florets around a prominent raised cone.
habitatDescription: Most common in open woodlands, prairie edges, savannas, and long-established plantings.
notes: Widely planted but also native, so it can appear both in remnants and landscaped restorations.

images.caption: Purple coneflower bloom on an official USFWS species media page.
Close-up flower detail of eastern purple coneflower.
Seed head / fruit of eastern purple coneflower.
Leaf detail of eastern purple coneflower.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: A straight wand of purple flower heads atop an upright stem gives this prairie plant its strong vertical look.
leafDescription: Leaves are narrow, grass-like, and densely spaced along the stem, becoming smaller higher up.
flowerDescription: Many fluffy purple flower heads are packed into a dense spike from the top downward.
habitatDescription: Most common in moist to mesic prairies, open meadows, and sunny roadside prairie restorations.
notes: Tall blazingstars are some of the best late-summer nectar plants for prairie insects.

images.caption: Prairie blazingstar blooming on prairie habitat.
Close-up flower detail of prairie blazingstar.
Seed head / fruit of prairie blazingstar.
Leaf detail of prairie blazingstar.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Huge rough basal leaves and a towering flowering stalk make this one of the most dramatic tallgrass prairie plants.
leafDescription: Basal leaves are enormous, heart-based, upright, and sandpapery, often held like paddles above the ground.
flowerDescription: Yellow sunflower-like heads are spaced along the very tall stalk well above the leaf rosette.
habitatDescription: A hallmark of deep-rooted prairie soils, often in full sun with little competition from woody plants.
notes: Even when not in flower, the giant basal leaves make this species recognizable from a distance.

images.caption: Prairie dock showing the towering flower stalk and large basal leaves.
Close-up flower detail of prairie dock.
Seed head / fruit of prairie dock.
Leaf detail of prairie dock.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Upright pink flower clusters and narrow opposite leaves point to this moisture-loving milkweed.
leafDescription: Leaves are narrow, smooth-edged, and opposite to whorled along the stem.
flowerDescription: Flowers form rounded pink clusters with a softer, more refined look than common milkweed.
habitatDescription: Best known from wetlands, swales, ditch edges, and consistently moist openings.
notes: A useful field contrast with butterfly milkweed because it signals wet ground rather than dry prairie.

images.caption: Swamp milkweed with pink flower clusters in wetland habitat.
Close-up flower detail of swamp milkweed.
Seed head / fruit of swamp milkweed.
Leaf detail of swamp milkweed.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: This aromatic mint has tufted lavender flower heads and unmistakable square stems.
leafDescription: Leaves are opposite, softly toothed, and noticeably fragrant when crushed.
flowerDescription: The flower heads are shaggy, with many narrow tubular florets gathered into rounded clusters.
habitatDescription: Frequent in sunny prairies and open edges where soils range from dry to moderately moist.
notes: A practical field clue is the mint-family stem shape combined with the loose pom-pom flower heads.

images.caption: Wild bergamot with shaggy lavender flower heads.
Close-up flower detail of wild bergamot.
Seed head / fruit of wild bergamot.
Leaf detail of wild bergamot.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Nodding red-and-yellow flowers with long backward spurs make this spring native immediately distinctive.
leafDescription: Leaves are delicate and divided into rounded lobed leaflets that give the plant a soft texture.
flowerDescription: The pendant flowers combine red outer parts with yellow petals and long nectar spurs.
habitatDescription: Most often seen on rocky wooded slopes, open bluffs, and lightly shaded savanna edges.
notes: This is one of the earliest showy native flowers in many upland natural areas.

images.caption: Wild columbine with red-and-yellow pendant flowers.
Close-up flower detail of wild columbine.
Seed head / fruit of wild columbine.
Leaf detail of wild columbine.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: A stout milkweed with broad leaves, milky sap, and heavy rounded clusters of dusty pink flowers.
leafDescription: Leaves are broad, opposite, thick, and pale beneath, often giving the plant a substantial, almost shrubby look.
flowerDescription: The flower clusters are dense and fragrant, usually pink to pink-purple and held near the upper stem.
habitatDescription: Common in old fields, roadsides, prairie edges, and other sunny places with moderately dry soil.
notes: Its big leaves and coarse habit separate it quickly from swamp and butterfly milkweed.

images.caption: Common milkweed blooming at Waubay National Wildlife Refuge.
Close-up flower detail of common milkweed.
Seed head / fruit of common milkweed.
Leaf detail of common milkweed.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Flat-topped yellow umbels and divided parsley-like leaves define this dependable spring prairie species.
leafDescription: Leaves are compound with broad toothed leaflets, especially noticeable on the lower plant.
flowerDescription: Small bright yellow flowers are grouped into umbrella-like clusters at the ends of branching stems.
habitatDescription: Widely found in moist prairies, meadows, and open edges, often flowering before the taller prairie plants wake up.
notes: A strong early nectar plant and a good visual marker for late spring in sunny remnants.

images.caption: Golden Alexanders (Zizia aurea) with yellow umbel flowers.
Close-up flower detail of golden alexanders.
Seed head / fruit of golden alexanders.
Leaf detail of golden alexanders.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Tall blue flower spikes and toothed leaves mark this wet-ground lobelia in late summer.
leafDescription: Leaves are alternate, broad to ovate, and sharply toothed, often along a stout upright stem.
flowerDescription: Flowers are larger and bluer than those of cardinal flower, packed along the upper stem in a dense spike.
habitatDescription: Found in moist openings, floodplain edges, wet meadows, and stream-adjacent thickets.
notes: The color shift from red cardinal flower to blue great blue lobelia is a useful late-season wetland contrast.

images.caption: Great blue lobelia with large blue flowers in a late-summer spike.
Close-up flower detail of great blue lobelia.
Seed head / fruit of great blue lobelia.
Leaf detail of great blue lobelia.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Soft blue nodding bells rising above smooth gray-green leaves make this a classic spring floodplain wildflower.
leafDescription: Leaves are smooth, oval, and slightly glaucous, giving the plant a cool blue-green tone even before bloom.
flowerDescription: Pink buds open to clear blue bell-shaped flowers that hang in loose clusters.
habitatDescription: Usually found in rich floodplain woods and moist bottomland forests before the tree canopy fully leafs out.
notes: The foliage fades quickly after flowering, so it is easiest to find during the short spring display window.

images.caption: Virginia bluebells in bloom on a spring woodland plant.
Close-up flower detail of virginia bluebells.
Seed head / fruit of virginia bluebells.
Leaf detail of virginia bluebells.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Rounded five-petaled pink flowers and deeply divided palmate leaves identify this common woodland spring perennial.
leafDescription: Leaves are palmately cut into broad segments, giving the plant a hand-shaped outline.
flowerDescription: Flowers are pink to lavender with five rounded petals and a neat, open face.
habitatDescription: Seen most often in deciduous woods, shaded slopes, and forest-edge openings with moderate moisture.
notes: The divided leaves make it recognizable even before the first blooms open.

images.caption: Wild geranium with purple petals and green leaves.
Close-up flower detail of wild geranium.
Seed head / fruit of wild geranium.
Leaf detail of wild geranium.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Umbrella-like leaves on paired stems create dense spring colonies on rich woodland floors.
leafDescription: Leaves are large, peltate, and deeply lobed, usually one on sterile stems and two on flowering stems.
flowerDescription: A single white flower hangs beneath the forked leaves, so it is often hidden unless you look underneath the canopy.
habitatDescription: Most common in rich woods and bottomland forests with moist, shaded soils.
notes: The leaf architecture is usually easier to notice than the flower, especially from a standing position.

images.caption: Mayapple colony in bloom with umbrella-like leaves.
Close-up flower detail of mayapple.
Seed head / fruit of mayapple.
Leaf detail of mayapple.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: A single clear white flower opens above a tightly wrapped blue-green leaf very early in spring.
leafDescription: Each stem supports one deeply lobed basal leaf that unfurls after flowering and stays longer than the bloom.
flowerDescription: The flower is bright white with many yellow stamens and usually opens on sunny spring days.
habitatDescription: Most abundant in rich deciduous woods, especially on slopes and streamside terraces.
notes: The plant emerges before most woodland herbs, making timing a big clue for identification.

images.caption: Bloodroot with a white spring flower and fresh leaf.
Close-up flower detail of bloodroot.
Seed head / fruit of bloodroot.
Leaf detail of bloodroot.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Its hooded green or purple-striped spathe with a central club-like spadix makes this woodland plant unmistakable.
leafDescription: Most plants show two large divided leaves, each usually with three broad leaflets.
flowerDescription: The true flowers are hidden inside the hooded spathe around the central spadix.
habitatDescription: Usually found in rich, moist woods and shaded floodplain ground with abundant leaf litter.
notes: This is a good reminder that not all showy spring plants have a typical open-petaled flower shape.

images.caption: Jack-in-the-pulpit showing the hooded spathe above woodland leaves.
Close-up flower detail of jack in the pulpit.
Seed head / fruit of jack in the pulpit.
Leaf detail of jack in the pulpit.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: This tall wetland composite is best known for opposite leaves that join around the stem like a cup.
leafDescription: Leaves are long, sharply toothed, and fused at the base so the stem appears to pierce them.
flowerDescription: The white flower heads are fuzzy and packed into broad flat-topped clusters.
habitatDescription: Most common in wet meadows, marshes, ditches, and other open ground with dependable summer moisture.
notes: The leaf arrangement is the fastest field clue, even before the white blooms open fully.

images.caption: Common boneset (Eupatorium perfoliatum) with white flower clusters.
Close-up flower detail of common boneset.
Seed head / fruit of common boneset.
Leaf detail of common boneset.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: A tall, broad-headed late-summer wildflower with mauve flower domes and whorled leaves.
leafDescription: Leaves are long, toothed, and usually arranged in whorls that circle the sturdy stem.
flowerDescription: Many small pink-purple flowers are packed into large rounded clusters near the top of the plant.
habitatDescription: Most common in wet meadows, prairie seeps, marsh edges, and tall herbaceous wetland communities.
notes: Even before flowering, the height and whorled leaves help separate it from shorter wetland asters and bonesets.

images.caption: Joe-Pye weed (Eutrochium sp.) with mauve flower clusters.
Close-up flower detail of joe pye weed.
Seed head / fruit of joe pye weed.
Leaf detail of joe pye weed.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Showy purple late-season daisy heads with yellow centers brighten wet meadows and restoration plantings in fall.
leafDescription: Leaves are narrow, clasping, and hairy, often giving the whole plant a rough look.
flowerDescription: The flower heads have many purple rays around yellow disks that age toward orange-brown.
habitatDescription: Most often planted or found in moist open ground, especially in restoration sites and roadside prairie seedings.
notes: A strong fall pollinator plant and one of the last conspicuous native composites before frost.

images.caption: New England aster in bloom with purple rays and yellow centers.
Close-up flower detail of new england aster.
Seed head / fruit of new england aster.
Leaf detail of new england aster.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Broad rough leaves and flat-topped yellow clusters give this goldenrod a chunky, sturdy appearance.
leafDescription: Lower leaves are broad and thick while upper leaves narrow but remain firm and often gray-green.
flowerDescription: Flower heads gather into broad, somewhat flattened clusters rather than long feathery wands.
habitatDescription: A common prairie goldenrod of sunny uplands, gravelly shoulders, and open savanna sites.
notes: Its flatter flower display is a useful contrast with the plume-like goldenrods often blamed for allergies.

images.caption: Stiff goldenrod (Solidago rigida) in bloom.
Close-up flower detail of stiff goldenrod.
Seed head / fruit of stiff goldenrod.
Leaf detail of stiff goldenrod.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Branched candelabra-like spikes of tiny blue flowers rise above toothed opposite leaves in wet open ground.
leafDescription: Leaves are opposite, lance-shaped, and sharply toothed, often with a slightly rough texture.
flowerDescription: Small blue-violet flowers open in rings along slender spikes that branch near the top.
habitatDescription: Most often seen in damp meadows, ditches, wet roadsides, and wet prairie openings.
notes: The spiky branching pattern makes it easy to separate from broad domed composites nearby.

images.caption: Blue vervain photographed at Neal Smith National Wildlife Refuge.
Close-up flower detail of blue vervain.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: A straight spike of snapdragon-like flowers and mint-family stems identify this prairie and wet-meadow perennial.
leafDescription: Leaves are opposite, narrow, and toothed, arranged along a sturdy square stem.
flowerDescription: Flowers are tubular and snapdragon-like, arranged in neat rows along an upright spike.
habitatDescription: Grows in moist prairie, low meadows, and alluvial ground where the soil stays moderately rich and open.
notes: The common name comes from the way individual flowers can stay bent briefly after being moved.

images.caption: Obedient plant with pink-purple flowers arranged along an upright spike.
Close-up flower detail of obedient plant.
Seed head / fruit of obedient plant.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Huge opposite leaves fused around the stem create water-holding cups beneath the tall yellow flower heads.
leafDescription: Leaves are broad, rough, and joined at the base so that rainwater collects around the stem.
flowerDescription: Bright yellow sunflower-like heads appear on many branches near the top of the towering plant.
habitatDescription: Most often rooted in moist prairie, floodplain openings, and rich low meadows.
notes: The leaf cup is the fastest field clue and often attracts insects or tiny frogs after rain.

images.caption: Cup plant (Silphium perfoliatum) showing yellow flowers and paired leaf cups.
Close-up flower detail of cup plant.
Seed head / fruit of cup plant.
Leaf detail of cup plant.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Deeply lobed basal leaves and a towering coarse stalk with yellow heads make this a signature tallgrass species.
leafDescription: Basal leaves are huge, deeply cut, and often held in a north-south orientation in exposed prairie settings.
flowerDescription: Yellow sunflower-like heads bloom along the upper stalk on short lateral branches.
habitatDescription: Common in open prairie and other full-sun upland remnants with deep, well-drained soil.
notes: Even out of flower, the giant lobed basal leaves make this plant easy to call in the field.

images.caption: Compass plant (Silphium laciniatum) with deeply lobed basal leaves and yellow flowers.
Close-up flower detail of compass plant.
Seed head / fruit of compass plant.
Leaf detail of compass plant.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Yucca-like leaves and round bristly white flower heads give this prairie plant an unusual, almost desert look.
leafDescription: Leaves are long, narrow, and parallel-sided with fibrous-looking edges and a strong vertical stance.
flowerDescription: The flower heads are spherical and pale, made of many tiny florets with spiny bracts.
habitatDescription: Best known from sunny prairie and open limestone or gravelly sites with little shade.
notes: It is one of the easiest prairie plants to identify from foliage alone before flowering.

images.caption: Rattlesnake master (Eryngium yuccifolium) with globe-shaped flower heads and yucca-like leaves.
Close-up flower detail of rattlesnake master.
Seed head / fruit of rattlesnake master.
Leaf detail of rattlesnake master.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Tall spires of narrow white flower spikes rise above whorled leaves in midsummer prairie and meadow habitats.
leafDescription: Leaves are arranged in noticeable whorls and are narrow, toothed, and pointed.
flowerDescription: Many small white flowers line several tapering spikes that create a chandelier-like top.
habitatDescription: Most common in moist prairie, low meadows, and sunny openings with good soil moisture but not standing water.
notes: The whorled leaves plus narrow white spikes separate it quickly from asters and bonesets.

images.caption: Culver's root (Veronicastrum virginicum) with tall white flower spikes.
Close-up flower detail of culvers root.
Seed head / fruit of culvers root.
Leaf detail of culvers root.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: Tall coarse stems topped with deep purple fuzzy flower clusters make ironweed a dramatic late-summer wetland plant.
leafDescription: Leaves are narrow, long, and strongly pointed with obvious teeth and a firm texture.
flowerDescription: Clusters of rich purple flower heads gather at the top of the plant without any petal-like rays.
habitatDescription: Most often seen in moist prairies, wet meadows, and open low ground that stays fairly sunny.
notes: The color is much darker and richer than most asters, and the stems keep a very upright habit.

images.caption: Ironweed (Vernonia fasciculata) with deep purple flower clusters.
Close-up flower detail of ironweed.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: A wetland shrub with opposite leaves, flat white flower clusters, and striking red stems, especially visible outside bloom.
leafDescription: Leaves are opposite, oval, and smooth-edged with the characteristic arcing dogwood veins.
flowerDescription: Small white flowers gather into flat-topped clusters that later mature into pale berries.
habitatDescription: Most often found on wet ground, especially along streambanks, slough edges, and low shrubby thickets.
notes: Even in winter the red twigs make this species easy to recognize, so it extends the guide beyond flowering season.

images.caption: Red osier dogwood (Cornus sericea) showing white flower clusters.
Close-up flower detail of red osier dogwood.
Seed head / fruit of red osier dogwood.
Leaf detail of red osier dogwood.

factSourceNotes: Referenced sources support factual verification only. Plant descriptions in this app are original manual paraphrases and should not be treated as copied source text.
identificationDescription: A vigorous climbing vine with five-part leaves and adhesive tendrils that allow it to cover trees, fences, and walls.
leafDescription: Leaves are palmately compound with five toothed leaflets radiating from one point.
flowerDescription: Flowers are small and greenish, later giving way to blue berries on red stalks.
habitatDescription: Occurs in woods, hedgerows, disturbed ground, and many urban edges where it can climb or sprawl.
notes: The five leaflets are the quickest separation from poison ivy, which usually has three.

images.caption: Virginia creeper (Parthenocissus quinquefolia) climbing a pin oak showing five-leaflet pattern.
Close-up flower detail of virginia creeper.
Seed head / fruit of virginia creeper.
Leaf detail of virginia creeper.
